walkthrough.title: Choose Player Colors
walkthrough.text: Let's get Cosmic Encounter set up. Each player picks a color. You will find five sets of player components in the box: red, blue, green, yellow, and purple. Each color comes with twenty ships and five planet discs. Grab your color and set the other components aside.

walkthrough.title: Assemble Home Systems
walkthrough.text: Now let's build your home system. Each player takes their five planet discs and arranges them in a cluster in front of them. Then place four of your ships on each of your five planets, standing upright. That is twenty ships total spread evenly across your five home planets. These are the colonies you need to defend.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Warp
walkthrough.text: Find the Warp disc and place it in the center of the table where everyone can reach it. The Warp is where ships go when they lose an encounter or get zapped. Think of it as a graveyard for defeated ships. Ships in the Warp are out of play until you retrieve them.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Hyperspace Gate
walkthrough.text: Place the hyperspace gate near the Warp. This cone-shaped piece is where you will place your attacking ships during an encounter. When the Destiny card tells you who to attack, you will point the gate at one of that player's planets and load your ships into it.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Cosmic Deck
walkthrough.text: Find the large deck of cosmic cards. These include encounter cards, which are numbered attack cards and negotiate cards. They also include artifact cards and reinforcement cards. Shuffle the entire cosmic deck thoroughly and place it face down in the center of the table near the Warp.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Hands
walkthrough.text: Deal eight cosmic cards face down to each player. Pick up your hand and look at it, but keep it hidden from other players. Your hand is your ammunition for encounters. If at any point you have no encounter cards in your hand, you will get to discard and draw a new hand of eight.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Destiny Deck
walkthrough.text: Now find the destiny deck. These are the smaller cards with colored swirls matching the player colors. Remove any colors not being used in your game. For example, in a four-player game, remove the fifth color's destiny cards. Shuffle the destiny deck and place it face down near the cosmic deck.

walkthrough.title: Select the Flare Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the flare cards in the cosmic deck. These are special cards tied to specific alien powers. For each alien in the game, find its matching flare and shuffle those flares into the cosmic deck. For your first game, you can skip this step and leave flares out entirely. They add fun variety but are easier to learn after your first game.

walkthrough.title: Deal Alien Powers
walkthrough.text: Now for the most exciting part. Shuffle the alien power cards and deal two face down to each player. Look at your two aliens, then choose one to keep and return the other face down to the box without showing anyone. Your alien power is your unique ability that bends or breaks the normal rules of the game.

walkthrough.title: Reveal Alien Powers
walkthrough.text: Once everyone has chosen, all players reveal their alien power cards simultaneously. Place your alien card face up in front of you so everyone can see it. Read each alien power aloud so the whole table understands what everyone can do. Take a moment to discuss how each power works. There are no secrets here — everyone should know what they are up against.

walkthrough.title: Understand Your Alien
walkthrough.text: Each alien card has a title, a power description, and a timing indicator that tells you when the power activates. Some powers are mandatory, meaning you must use them. Others are optional. The card text always overrides the normal rules. That is the core magic of Cosmic Encounter — your alien lets you break the game in a specific way.

walkthrough.title: Select First Player
walkthrough.text: Almost ready. Choose a starting player at random. The rulebook suggests the player with the birthday closest to today, but any method works. Play will proceed clockwise from the starting player. Each player gets a full turn with potentially two encounters before play passes to the next person. Let's learn the rules.

summary.title: Select First Player
bullets: Choose starting player randomly
Play proceeds clockwise
Each player may have up to 2 encounters per turn

walkthrough.title: The Goal
walkthrough.text: Let's learn how to play Cosmic Encounter. The goal is simple: be the first player to establish five foreign colonies. A foreign colony is any planet belonging to another player where you have at least one ship. Your own home planets do not count toward this goal. You can share a win if multiple players reach five foreign colonies at the same time.

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure Overview
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you go through the encounter sequence. An encounter has six phases that always happen in the same order: Regroup, Destiny, Launch, Alliance, Planning, and Reveal. After resolving the encounter, if you won as the offense, you may have a second encounter. If you lost or did not gain a colony, your turn ends and play passes clockwise. You can never have more than two encounters per turn.

walkthrough.title: Phase One: Regroup
walkthrough.text: If you have any ships in the Warp, take one and place it on any of your home colonies. Just one ship, every turn. If the Warp is empty or you have no ships there, simply skip this step. This is the only reliable way to recover lost ships, so never forget it.

walkthrough.title: Phase Two: Destiny
walkthrough.text: Now draw the top card of the destiny deck. This card tells you who you must encounter. If the card shows a player's color, you must attack that player. You cannot choose a different target. If you draw your own color, draw again. Some destiny cards show special icons instead of colors, and these have their own rules explained in the appendix. The destiny deck ensures you can't just bully one player all game.

walkthrough.title: Phase Three: Launch
walkthrough.text: Point the hyperspace gate at one of the target player's planets. You choose which of their planets to attack, not which player. Then take one to four ships from any of your colonies and place them on the hyperspace gate. These are your attacking force. You must send at least one ship, and you can send a maximum of four. Choose carefully — every ship you commit is a ship leaving your existing colonies undefended.

walkthrough.title: Phase Four: Alliance
walkthrough.text: Now both the offense and the defense may invite allies. The offense asks first. Point at anyone at the table and say something like, 'Will you ally with me?' Then the defense gets to ask for allies. Each invited player chooses whether to accept or decline. Allies commit one to four ships from their colonies to the encounter. Allied ships go on the gate for the offense or on the targeted planet for the defense.

walkthrough.title: Why Alliances Matter
walkthrough.text: Allies share in the outcome. If you allied with the offense and the offense wins, you also land ships on the target planet, gaining a foreign colony. If you allied with the defense and the defense wins, you get rewards — you draw cards from the cosmic deck or retrieve ships from the Warp. But if your side loses, all allied ships go to the Warp. Alliances are risky, rewarding, and the social heart of the game.

walkthrough.title: Phase Five: Planning
walkthrough.text: The offense and defense each select one card from their hand and place it face down. Only encounter cards are valid here — attack cards and negotiate cards. You cannot play an artifact or reinforcement as your encounter card. This is the moment of tension. Both players stare each other down, trying to guess what the other person played. No one else can see the cards until the reveal.

walkthrough.title: Phase Six: Reveal
walkthrough.text: Both players flip their encounter cards face up at the same time. What happens next depends on what was played. There are three possible scenarios: Attack versus Attack, Negotiate versus Negotiate, or one of each. Let's go through each scenario.

walkthrough.title: Attack vs Attack Resolution
walkthrough.text: When both players reveal attack cards, it is a fight. Each side totals their score: the number on their attack card plus the number of ships on their side, including allies. The higher total wins. If there is a tie, the defense wins. The losing side's ships, including all allied ships, go to the Warp. The winning offensive ships land on the planet as a new colony. Reinforcement cards can be played after the reveal to add to either side's total.

walkthrough.title: Negotiate vs Negotiate Resolution
walkthrough.text: When both players reveal negotiate cards, a deal must happen. All allied ships on both sides return to their owners' colonies — allies are dismissed. The two main players now have one minute to make a deal. Deals can involve trading cards, granting colonies, or anything the two players agree on. If they reach a deal, both carry it out immediately. If they fail to agree within one minute, both players lose three ships each to the Warp.

walkthrough.title: Attack vs Negotiate Resolution
walkthrough.text: When one player reveals an attack and the other reveals a negotiate, the attacker wins automatically. The negotiator's ships go to the Warp. However, the losing negotiator gets compensation: they draw cards from the winning player's hand, one card per ship they lost. This is a consolation prize that can be very valuable — you might snag their best attack cards.

walkthrough.title: Gaining Colonies
walkthrough.text: When the offense wins an encounter, all offensive ships on the gate land on the target planet. Those ships are now a foreign colony. The defensive ships that were already on that planet stay there too — multiple players can coexist on the same planet. Each planet you have ships on counts as one foreign colony, no matter how many ships you have there. Remember, you need five foreign colonies to win.

walkthrough.title: Second Encounter
walkthrough.text: If you won your first encounter as the offense and gained a colony, you may choose to have a second encounter this turn. Draw a new destiny card and go through the full encounter sequence again. If you win the second encounter too, your turn ends — no third encounter is allowed. If you lost your first encounter, your turn ends immediately with no second chance.

walkthrough.title: Running Out of Cards
walkthrough.text: If at any point you have no encounter cards in your hand, you must reveal your hand to prove it. Then discard your entire hand and draw a fresh hand of eight cards from the cosmic deck. This can happen mid-turn or between turns. If the cosmic deck runs out, shuffle the discard pile to form a new deck. You always need encounter cards to participate.

walkthrough.title: Alien Powers in Play
walkthrough.text: Your alien power is active throughout the game unless something specific turns it off. Some aliens change how encounters resolve, others give you extra turns or special resources. Always read the power text carefully — it tells you exactly when and how it works. Alien powers can override any rule we have discussed. If the power says you can do something the rules normally forbid, the power wins. That is the beauty of Cosmic Encounter.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: The game ends the moment any player reaches five foreign colonies. This can happen during your own encounter, as an ally helping the offense, or even during someone else's turn through a deal. If multiple players reach five colonies at the same time, they all share the victory. Shared wins are a huge part of the social dynamics. Sometimes the smartest play is to help another player win alongside you rather than going for it alone.

walkthrough.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a few scripted encounters with five players: Red, Blue, Green, Yellow, and Purple. Each player has a unique alien power, and one player will make some common mistakes so we can all learn from them. Follow along even if you have fewer players. Let's jump in.

walkthrough.title: Starting Positions
walkthrough.text: Everyone has their five home planets with four ships each, a hand of eight cosmic cards, and their alien power revealed. Take a moment to look at your hand. You should see a mix of attack cards with different numbers, maybe a negotiate card, and possibly a reinforcement or artifact card. The attack cards are your weapons, and the negotiate cards are your diplomacy tools.

walkthrough.title: Red's Turn: Regroup Phase
walkthrough.text: Red goes first. In the Regroup phase, Red can retrieve one ship from the Warp. But it is the first turn, so no ships are in the Warp yet. Red skips Regroup and moves straight to the Destiny phase.

walkthrough.title: Red's Turn: Destiny Phase
walkthrough.text: Red draws a destiny card and it shows Blue. That means Red must encounter Blue — no choice in the matter. Red cannot decide to attack Green or Yellow instead. The destiny deck keeps the game fair by making sure everyone gets targeted eventually.

walkthrough.title: Red's Turn: Launch Phase
walkthrough.text: Red points the hyperspace gate at one of Blue's planets. Now Red needs to choose how many ships to send. Let's say Red loads four ships onto the gate from one of their home planets. That is the maximum, and it gives Red the best chance of winning the encounter. But notice — that home planet now has zero ships on it. It is undefended.

walkthrough.title: Red's Mistake: Over-Committing Ships
walkthrough.text: Red left that home planet completely empty. If anyone attacks that planet later, they take it automatically with no fight. A smarter play is to spread your ships across multiple colonies, taking two ships from one planet and two from another. That way Red still sends four ships but no single planet is left totally exposed.

walkthrough.title: Red's Turn: Alliance Phase
walkthrough.text: Now Red can invite allies. Red looks around the table and asks Green, 'Will you ally with me?' Green thinks about it. If Green allies with the offense and they win, Green's ships also land on Blue's planet, earning Green a free foreign colony. Green decides to commit two ships. Red does not invite anyone else. Now Blue gets to invite allies, and asks Yellow for help. Yellow commits one ship to the defense.

walkthrough.title: Red's Turn: Planning Phase
walkthrough.text: Red and Blue each pick one encounter card from their hand and place it face down. This is the tense moment. Red has a nice attack twelve in hand and plays it confidently. Blue looks through their cards and plays an attack eight. Both cards are face down and nobody knows what the other played yet.

walkthrough.title: Red's Turn: Reveal and Resolution
walkthrough.text: Both players flip their cards. Red reveals attack twelve. Blue reveals attack eight. Now we count totals. Red has twelve plus four ships on the gate plus Green's two allied ships, for a total of eighteen. Blue has eight plus four ships on the planet plus Yellow's one allied ship, for a total of thirteen. Red wins! Blue and Yellow's ships on that planet go to the Warp. Red and Green's ships land on the planet, each earning a foreign colony.

walkthrough.title: Understanding the Outcome
walkthrough.text: Let's break down what just happened. Red and Green each gained one foreign colony, moving closer to five. Blue lost four ships and Yellow lost one ship to the Warp. Blue still has ships on their other four planets, so they are not out of the game. But those five ships in the Warp will take several turns to recover at one ship per Regroup phase.

walkthrough.title: Red's Second Encounter
walkthrough.text: Red won the first encounter, so Red gets a second encounter this turn. Red draws another destiny card and it shows Purple. Red points the gate at one of Purple's planets and sends three ships this time, leaving at least one ship on each of their occupied planets. Red learned from the earlier over-commitment mistake.

walkthrough.title: Blue's Turn: A Negotiate Play
walkthrough.text: Red's turn is over. Now it is Blue's turn. Blue retrieves one ship from the Warp during Regroup. Blue draws a destiny card showing Green. Blue sends three ships to attack one of Green's planets. During the Alliance phase, nobody volunteers to help either side. In the Planning phase, both Blue and Green play their cards face down.

walkthrough.title: Blue's Negotiate Resolution
walkthrough.text: Reveal time. Blue played a negotiate card and Green played an attack card. When attack beats negotiate, the attacker wins automatically — Green wins and Blue's ships go to the Warp. But Blue gets compensation: one card drawn from Green's hand for each ship lost. Blue lost three ships, so Blue draws three cards from Green's hand. Blue does not get to look at Green's hand first — just draw three at random.

walkthrough.title: Green's Mistake: Ignoring Negotiation
walkthrough.text: Now it is Green's turn. Green draws destiny and gets Yellow. Green looks at their hand, sees an attack twenty, and is eager to play it. Green sends four ships and refuses all alliance offers. Green just wants to smash the encounter with brute force. But Yellow plays negotiate. Green wins automatically but look at the cost — Yellow draws four cards from Green's hand as compensation. Green's hand is now dangerously thin.

walkthrough.title: The Negotiation Lesson
walkthrough.text: Green made a classic mistake: playing purely based on card math and ignoring the social side of the game. If Green had also played negotiate, both players could have made a deal — maybe trading colonies or cards in a way that benefits both. Cosmic Encounter is not just about having the biggest numbers. The best players use diplomacy, bluffing, and deals to advance their position while saving their powerful cards for critical moments.

walkthrough.title: Yellow's Turn: Using an Alien Power
walkthrough.text: Yellow's turn now. Let's say Yellow has the Oracle power, which lets Yellow look at their opponent's encounter card before choosing their own. Yellow draws destiny and gets Red. During the Planning phase, Red plays a card face down first, and then Yellow peeks at it — it is an attack six. Knowing this, Yellow plays an attack ten and wins easily. That is the beauty of alien powers. They change the entire way you approach each encounter.

walkthrough.title: Purple's Mistake: Ignoring Their Alien Power
walkthrough.text: Purple's turn. Let's say Purple has the Trader power, which lets Purple swap hands with their opponent. Purple draws destiny and gets Blue. Instead of using the Trader power, Purple just plays a mid-range attack card and barely wins. Purple gained a colony, but missed a huge opportunity. Blue had a much better hand after all that compensation from earlier encounters. If Purple had swapped hands, Purple would have upgraded their entire position.

walkthrough.title: The Alien Power Lesson
walkthrough.text: The lesson here is critical: your alien power is your biggest advantage. Do not play generic turns when your power gives you a special edge. Every turn, ask yourself how your alien ability changes the situation. Build your entire strategy around your power. A player who uses their alien effectively will beat a player with better cards every time.

walkthrough.title: The Alliance Dynamic
walkthrough.text: As we continue, notice how alliances shape the game. When Red invited Green as an ally earlier, Green got a free colony without being the active player. This is huge. Offensive alliances are the sneakiest way to gain colonies because you do not have to spend your own encounter cards. Always consider joining as an ally when invited — free colonies are the fastest path to five.

walkthrough.title: Watching the Colony Count
walkthrough.text: Let's check the scoreboard after one full round. Red has two foreign colonies from winning both encounters. Green has one colony from allying with Red. Yellow has one colony from beating Red. Purple has one colony from beating Blue. Blue has zero foreign colonies and several ships in the Warp. The game is wide open — anyone can still win, and alliances will determine who gets there first.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen all six encounter phases in action, watched three common mistakes play out, and learned why ship management, negotiation, and alien powers each matter. Remember: do not over-commit ships, do not ignore the social game, and always leverage your alien power. You are ready to play on your own.

walkthrough.title: Before You Launch
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to encounter phases, card types, and alien powers.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Launch
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → encounter phases, card types, aliens
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, here are the biggest mistakes new players make. First, over-committing ships to a single encounter and leaving home planets empty. Second, ignoring negotiation and diplomacy by treating every encounter as pure combat. Third, not using your alien power to its full potential. Fourth, failing to track other players' colony counts — someone will sneak to five if you are not paying attention. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest beginner traps.

walkthrough.title: Ship Management
walkthrough.text: Your twenty ships are a finite resource, and managing them is the most important skill in Cosmic Encounter. Never strip a planet bare to send four ships on offense unless the stakes are critical. Two or three ships per encounter is usually enough, especially when you have allies adding to your total. Keep at least one ship on every home planet so opponents cannot take free colonies. Ships in the Warp recover slowly — just one per turn — so losing five ships in a bad encounter sets you back for many turns.

walkthrough.title: The Social Game
walkthrough.text: Cosmic Encounter is a negotiation game disguised as a space combat game. Every alliance invitation, every deal, every table talk conversation is a chance to advance your position. Invite allies when you need extra firepower, but remember that offensive allies gain colonies too. Sometimes it is better to go it alone so nobody else benefits from your win. On defense, actively recruit allies — the more ships on your side, the harder you are to beat. And always be willing to negotiate. A deal that gives both players something is better than a fight where someone loses ships.

walkthrough.title: Card Hand Management
walkthrough.text: Your hand of cosmic cards is your lifeline. High attack cards are precious — do not waste them on encounters that do not matter. Save your twenty, thirty, and forty attack cards for encounters where gaining or losing a colony would significantly affect the game. Use your low cards to bluff or throw away on encounters you expect to lose anyway. Negotiate cards are extremely valuable in the right situations — playing one against an attack card gets you compensation cards drawn from your opponent's hand. And when your hand gets low on encounter cards, that is actually an opportunity. You get to discard everything and draw a fresh eight.

walkthrough.title: Alliance Strategy
walkthrough.text: Alliances are the fastest path to victory. When someone invites you as an offensive ally, you gain a colony if the offense wins, without spending any encounter cards from your hand. That is an incredible deal. Accept offensive alliance invitations liberally in the early game to rack up colonies. On defense, allies help you survive by adding ships to your total, and they get rewards like cards or ships from the Warp. Be selective about defensive alliances later in the game though — you do not want to help someone who is about to win.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Table
walkthrough.text: Always keep track of how many foreign colonies each player has. When someone reaches three or four colonies, the table needs to rally against them. Stop inviting that player as an offensive ally. Ally against them on defense. Encourage other players to do the same. The player who is closest to winning should face maximum resistance. Conversely, if you are close to winning, try to stay under the radar. Downplay your position, make deals that benefit others, and strike when the table is not paying attention.

walkthrough.title: Embrace Your Alien
walkthrough.text: Your alien power should shape every decision you make. If you have a combat-focused alien, lean into aggressive encounters and strong attack cards. If you have a diplomacy-focused alien, seek out negotiations and deals. If your alien gives you an economic advantage, use it to build a stronger hand or recover ships faster. Do not play a generic game — the alien powers exist to make each player's strategy unique. Read your power card again every few turns to make sure you are not missing opportunities.

walkthrough.title: Winning Through Shared Victories
walkthrough.text: Do not overlook shared wins. If you are at four colonies and another player is also at four, you might be able to coordinate a joint attack where you both reach five simultaneously. This is perfectly legal and a legitimate strategy. Sometimes the smartest play is to offer another player a ride to victory alongside you rather than fighting alone and losing. Propose shared wins openly — it shifts the table dynamics and forces other players to decide who they want to stop. Just watch out for backstabs, because not every handshake deal sticks.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't over-commit ships — empty planets are free colonies for opponents
Don't ignore negotiation — every encounter has a social angle
Don't neglect your alien power — it's your biggest edge
Don't stop tracking colony counts — someone will sneak to 5

summary.title: Ship Management
bullets: 20 ships are finite — never strip a planet bare unless critical
2-3 ships per encounter usually enough with allies
Keep at least 1 ship on every home planet
Warp recovery is slow (1/turn) — losing 5 ships = major setback

summary.title: The Social Game
bullets: Negotiation game disguised as space combat
Invite allies for firepower, but offense allies gain colonies too
Recruit defense allies to boost your total
A deal benefiting both sides beats a fight where someone loses ships

summary.title: Card Hand Management
bullets: Save high attack cards (20, 30, 40) for critical encounters
Use low cards on encounters you expect to lose
Negotiate cards give compensation (cards from opponent's hand)
Empty hand = discard all, draw fresh 8 — sometimes an advantage

summary.title: Alliance Strategy
bullets: Offensive alliances: gain colonies without spending encounter cards
Accept offense invitations liberally in early game
Defensive alliances: add ships to your total, allies get card/Warp rewards
Late game: don't ally with players close to winning

summary.title: Reading the Table
bullets: Track every player's foreign colony count at all times
3-4 colonies → table should rally against that player
Stop inviting leading players as offense allies
If you're close to winning, stay under the radar

summary.title: Embrace Your Alien
bullets: Let your alien power shape every decision
Combat alien → aggressive; diplomacy alien → deals; economy alien → resources
Re-read your power card every few turns for missed opportunities
Don't play generic — powers make each strategy unique

summary.title: Winning Through Shared Victories
bullets: Shared wins are legal and a legitimate strategy
Coordinate with another player at 4 colonies to both hit 5
Proposing shared wins shifts table dynamics
Watch for backstabs — not every handshake sticks

walkthrough.title: Ship Economy Mastery
walkthrough.text: Advanced players treat ships as currency, not just soldiers. Every ship has value: it is a defender on your planets, a contributor to encounter totals, and a potential colony if it lands on foreign soil. The key insight is that ships committed to offense have the highest upside because they can become colonies, while ships on defense just maintain the status quo. Prioritize offensive ship allocation when you need colonies, and consolidate defensively when protecting a lead.

walkthrough.title: Card Counting and Hand Reading
walkthrough.text: The cosmic deck has a known distribution of attack cards. The highest card is attack forty, and there are very few cards above twenty. If an opponent has played two high attack cards already, they probably do not have another one. Track what gets played and what gets discarded. When an opponent's hand gets small, they are more likely to play negotiate for the compensation draw. Use this information to decide when to play big and when to sandbag with a low card.

walkthrough.title: The Negotiate Meta-Game
walkthrough.text: Negotiate cards are far more powerful than new players realize. Playing negotiate against an attack guarantees you compensation — drawing cards from your opponent's hand. Against a player with a great hand, this is theft. And if both players negotiate, the resulting deal can be tailored to benefit you more than a combat win would. Advanced players will bluff negotiate intentions to pressure opponents into negotiations, then play attack to steal the win. The threat of a negotiate is almost as powerful as the card itself.

walkthrough.title: Timing Your Power Plays
walkthrough.text: Do not rush toward five colonies. A player at four colonies becomes everyone's target, and the table will unite to stop them. The ideal approach is to stay at two or three colonies until the mid-game, then sprint to five in one or two turns using a combination of encounters and alliances. Set up alliances in advance — agree to help someone on their turn if they help you on yours. Coordinate back-to-back turns where you and an ally both reach five colonies simultaneously.

walkthrough.title: Defensive Colony Consolidation
walkthrough.text: When you have colonies on enemy planets, those ships are also targets. If an opponent attacks a planet where you have a foreign colony, your ships there are defending alongside the planet's owner. You can lose foreign colonies this way. Advanced players stack multiple ships on key foreign colonies to make them harder to dislodge, and they choose which planets to colonize strategically — planets with more defenders are safer long-term homes.

walkthrough.title: Alliance Manipulation
walkthrough.text: The alliance phase is the most strategically rich moment of each encounter. As the offense, invite allies selectively — you want enough firepower to win but not so many allies that everyone gains a free colony. Sometimes invite only one strong ally and promise them something in return. As the defense, recruit everyone — the more ships against the offense, the better. When you are neither offense nor defense, use alliance invitations as leverage. 'I will ally with you if you promise to ally with me on my next turn' is a classic deal that wins games.

walkthrough.title: Playing to Your Alien's Strengths
walkthrough.text: Each alien power warps the game in a specific way. The best players identify how their alien changes the fundamental math of encounters and exploit that ruthlessly. If your alien reduces risk, take more aggressive gambles. If it provides information, use that knowledge to play the minimum card needed to win. If it manipulates alliances, become the kingmaker at the table. Study the common alien powers before you play — knowing what other aliens do is just as important as knowing your own.

walkthrough.title: The End-Game Sprint
walkthrough.text: Most games of Cosmic Encounter are decided in two or three dramatic turns at the end. The player who wins is usually the one who conceals their position, sets up alliances, and then strikes in a coordinated rush. Ideally, you want to go from three colonies to five in a single turn: win your first encounter for colony four, then win your second encounter for colony five. Or better yet, win your encounter for colony four and gain colony five as an ally on someone else's turn before anyone can react. The end-game is all about timing, diplomacy, and one big push.

summary.title: Ship Economy Mastery
bullets: Ships are currency: defenders, encounter contributors, and potential colonies
Offensive ships have highest upside (can become colonies)
Defensive ships maintain status quo — less rewarding
Prioritize offense when colony-hunting, consolidate when leading

summary.title: Card Counting and Hand Reading
bullets: Track played attack cards — few cards above 20 in the deck
Opponent played 2 high cards? Likely out of big ones
Small hand → more likely to play negotiate for compensation
Use card knowledge to decide when to play big vs sandbag

summary.title: The Negotiate Meta-Game
bullets: Negotiate vs attack = compensation (steal cards from opponent's hand)
Both negotiate = custom deal, can be better than combat win
Bluff negotiate intentions to pressure opponents into deals
The threat of negotiate is almost as powerful as the card

summary.title: Timing Your Power Plays
bullets: Stay at 2-3 colonies mid-game — 4 colonies = everyone targets you
Sprint to 5 in 1-2 turns using encounters + alliances
Pre-arrange alliance trades: help me, I help you
Coordinate simultaneous wins for shared victories

summary.title: Defensive Colony Consolidation
bullets: Foreign colonies can be lost if that planet is attacked
Stack multiple ships on key colonies to make them harder to dislodge
Choose planets with more defenders for safer long-term colonies
Defending foreign colonies costs you ships if the fight is lost

summary.title: Alliance Manipulation
bullets: Offense: invite selectively — too many allies = free colonies for rivals
Defense: recruit everyone — more ships = harder to beat
Neutral: use alliance invitations as leverage for future deals
'I ally with you now if you ally with me next' wins games

summary.title: Playing to Your Alien's Strengths
bullets: Identify how your alien changes encounter math → exploit ruthlessly
Risk-reducing aliens → take aggressive gambles
Information aliens → play minimum card needed to win
Know other aliens' powers as well as your own

summary.title: The End-Game Sprint
bullets: Most games decided in 2-3 dramatic end-game turns
Ideal: go from 3 colonies → 5 in a single turn (2 encounters)
Or win encounter for #4, gain #5 as ally on someone else's turn
Conceal position, set up alliances, then strike in coordinated rush

entries.definition: The first phase of every encounter. The offense retrieves one ship from the Warp and places it on any of their home colonies. If no ships are in the Warp, skip this phase.
The second phase. Draw the top card of the destiny deck to determine which player you must encounter. You cannot choose a different target. If you draw your own color, draw again.
The third phase. Point the hyperspace gate at one of the target player's planets, then place one to four ships from your colonies onto the gate. You must send at least one ship.
The fourth phase. The offense invites allies first, then the defense invites allies. Invited players choose to accept or decline. Allies commit one to four ships from their own colonies.
The fifth phase. The offense and defense each select one encounter card from their hand and play it face down. Only attack cards and negotiate cards are valid encounter cards.
The sixth and final phase. Both encounter cards are flipped face up simultaneously. Resolution depends on the combination: attack vs attack, negotiate vs negotiate, or attack vs negotiate.
An encounter card with a numeric value (ranging from zero to forty). In combat, your total is the card value plus the number of ships on your side. Higher total wins; ties go to the defense.

An encounter card used for diplomacy. If both sides play negotiate, they must make a deal within one minute or both lose three ships. If played against an attack card, the negotiator loses but draws compensation cards from the winner's hand.
A card played after the reveal phase to add its value to either side's attack total. Can be played by the main players or their allies. Multiple reinforcement cards can be played on the same side.

A special one-time-use card that can be played at specific moments to create powerful effects. Each artifact has its own timing and effect described on the card. Artifacts are not encounter cards and cannot be played during the Planning phase.

A card tied to a specific alien power. Each flare has two effects: a wild effect usable by anyone, and a super effect usable only by the matching alien. Flares are shuffled into the cosmic deck and drawn like normal cards.

The main draw deck containing attack cards, negotiate cards, reinforcement cards, artifact cards, and flare cards. Players draw from this deck to replenish their hands. When depleted, reshuffle the discard pile.
A separate deck of colored cards that determines which player you must encounter on your turn. Contains cards for each player color in the game plus special destiny cards. Reshuffled when depleted.

Any planet belonging to another player where you have at least one ship. Each foreign colony counts as one toward the five needed to win, regardless of how many ships you have there. Your own home planets never count.
A colony on one of your own five home planets. Home colonies are where your ships start and where retrieved ships return. Losing all ships on a home planet means opponents can colonize it freely.
Multiple players can have ships on the same planet simultaneously. Each player with ships there has a colony. When the planet is attacked, all players with ships there defend together.

Plastic pieces representing your space fleet. Each player starts with twenty ships. Ships are placed on planets to form colonies, committed to encounters on the hyperspace gate, and sent to the Warp when defeated.

A central area where defeated ships are sent. Ships in the Warp are out of play. You retrieve one ship per turn during the Regroup phase. Some alien powers and card effects can retrieve additional ships.
The cone-shaped piece pointed at the target planet during an encounter. Offensive ships and offensive allied ships are placed on the gate. If the offense wins, these ships land on the target planet.

The active player whose turn it is, conducting the encounter. The offense draws the destiny card, chooses which planet to attack, and sends ships through the hyperspace gate. The offense invites allies first.
The player whose planet is being attacked. The defense has ships on the targeted planet and plays an encounter card against the offense. The defense invites allies after the offense.

A player who commits ships to the hyperspace gate to help the offense. If the offense wins, allied ships also land on the target planet, granting the ally a foreign colony. If the offense loses, allied ships go to the Warp.

A player who commits ships to the targeted planet to help the defense. If the defense wins, defensive allies receive rewards: they may draw cards from the cosmic deck or retrieve ships from the Warp. If the defense loses, allied ships go to the Warp.

When the defense wins, each defensive ally collects rewards equal to the number of ships they committed. For each ship, they may either draw one card from the cosmic deck or retrieve one ship from the Warp.

When a negotiate card loses to an attack card, the losing player draws cards at random from the winning player's hand. The number of cards drawn equals the number of ships the negotiator lost to the Warp.

If two or more players reach five foreign colonies at the same time — through encounters, alliances, or deals — they all win together. Shared victories are a legitimate and common outcome, especially in experienced groups.

A card describing a unique ability that overrides normal game rules. Each player has one alien power active throughout the game. Powers have specific timing, may be mandatory or optional, and always take precedence over base rules.
An alien power that must be used whenever its trigger condition occurs. The player cannot choose to skip it. Mandatory powers can sometimes work against the player depending on the situation.
An alien power that the player may choose to use or not when its trigger condition occurs. Optional powers give more flexibility and are generally considered easier to play.
The top half of a flare card, usable by any player regardless of their alien. Wild effects are typically weaker than super effects. After use, the flare is discarded.

The bottom half of a flare card, usable only by the player whose alien matches the flare. Super effects are powerful abilities that enhance the matching alien's theme. After use, the flare returns to the player's hand instead of being discarded.

An agreement made when both players reveal negotiate cards. Deals can include trading cards, exchanging colonies, promising future alliances, or anything both players agree on. Deals must involve something tangible — vague promises do not count.

When both players play negotiate but cannot reach an agreement within one minute, the deal fails. Both players lose three ships each to the Warp as a penalty. This makes negotiation risky but usually worthwhile.
